manipulated_prompt_obj.content:
Provide one single-word clue that relates to some of your team's words from the list below. You can choose to how many and to which words your clue relates to.
The goal is to have your partner guess all of your team's words based on the clues you give them, before the other team has uncovered all of theirs. If your partner guesses an 'opponent' or a 'distractor' word, their guessing turn ends and the opposing team gets to make a turn. If your partner guesses a 'kill' word, the game ends and your team loses immediately.
The more words your clue relates to, the quicker you can win the game, but the harder the associations may be to guess for your partner, so choose your clue word wisely. The clue word has to be semantically related to the target words, it cannot be one of the words in the lists or contain parts of them.

Always give your single-word clue and your comma-separated list of related target words in the following format and make your answers as short as possible, never include any other text than is required in this form:

CLUE: <WORD>
TARGETS: <TARGETS>

Your team words are: olive, mammoth, post, grace, superhero, dance, laser, pool, spring.
Your opponent's team words are: play, cotton, unicorn, europe, bill, scale, nurse, chocolate.
Distractor words are: charge, genius, mexico, paper, pound, circle, phoenix.
The kill words are: kiwi.

The goal is to have your partner guess all of your team's words based on the clues you give them, before the other team has uncovered all of theirs. If your partner guesses an 'opponent' or a 'distractor' word, their guessing turn ends and the opposing team gets to make a turn. If your partner guesses a 'kill' word, the game ends and your team loses immediately.
The more words your clue relates to, the quicker you can win the game, but the harder the associations may be to guess for your partner, so choose your clue word wisely. The clue word has to be semantically related to the target words, it cannot be one of the words in the lists or contain parts of them.

Always give your single-word clue and your comma-separated list of related target words in the following format and make your answers as short as possible, never include any other text than is required in this form:

The goal is to give a clue that relates to as many of the remaining team words as possible, while minimizing the risk of the opponent guessing opponent words, distractor words, or the kill word. The clue must be a single word, not one of the words in the list or contain parts of them.

I need to think of a single word that semantically relates to both "post" and "grace".

Let me think about "post":
- A post can be a pole or stake
- Post can refer to mail/postal service
- Post can mean to publish online
- Post can refer to a position/job (e.g., "the post of commander")
- Post can be a military installation
- Post can mean after (e.g., post-war)
- Post can be a blog post

Let me think about "grace":
- Grace can mean elegance, poise, refinement
- Grace can be a prayer before meals
- Grace can be mercy, clemency
- Grace can be a period of time (grace period)
- Grace can be a virtue
- Grace can be a name

Words that might connect both:
- "elegance" - grace has elegance, but post doesn't really
- "mail" - relates to post, but not grace
- "divine" - grace has religious connotations, but post doesn't
- "period" - grace period is a phrase, but post period? Not really
- "position" - a post is a position, grace... not really
- "prayer" - relates to grace (saying grace), but not post
- "eucharist" - relates to grace (saying grace), but not post
- "elegance" - grace is elegance, post is not

Wait, let me think differently. "Post" can mean a position or job. "Grace" can be a virtue or quality one brings to a position. So maybe "position" or "office" or "role"?

"Office":
- Post: a post is a position/office (e.g., "the post of secretary")
- Grace: can be associated with office (e.g., "grace of office", "office of grace" - might be a stretch)

Hmm, not great.

What about "dignity"?
- Grace has dignity, elegance
- Post: one can hold a post with dignity

"Service":
- Post: postal service, military service at a post
- Grace: divine grace, service to God
This could work.

"Estate":
- Post: a post can refer to a station (military post)
- Grace: state of grace
Not great.

"State":
- Post: to post something is to put it in a certain state
- Grace: state of grace
Could work, but "state" might also relate to "bill" (bill is proposed legislation, which relates to state), "europe" (states in Europe), "scale" (state of scale?), "paper" (state of paper?), "pound" (state currency). Actually "state" could relate to many opponent/distractor words: "europe" (European states), "bill" (state legislature), "paper" (state documents), "pound" (pound is a state currency in the UK). That's too risky.

"Order":
- Post: postal orders, or a post can be an order
- Grace: grace is orderly, elegant
But "order" could relate to "bill" (legislative order), "scale" (order of magnitude), "paper" (order paper), "pound" (ordered currency). Also could relate to "play" (play in order). Not ideal.

"Box":
- Post: post office box, mailbox
- Grace: doesn't relate well

"Peace":
- Post: post-war peace
- Grace: graceful peace
But "peace" might relate to many words.

"Period":
- Post: post can mean after, a period after
- Grace: grace period
Actually "grace period" is a very common phrase! And "post" can relate to period as in "post the period" or just that both relate to time concepts. But "period" could relate to "bill" (billing period), "scale" (period of scale), "play" (play period), "paper" (paper periodical). Not too bad though.

"Mark":
- Post: postmark (stamp on mail)
- Grace: grace marks (in academics), or to mark with grace
"Postmark" is a direct connection! And "grace" can be a mark of distinction.
